title: Values not convertible to numbers should be avoided in numeric comparisons
description: Values not convertible to numbers should not be used in numeric comparisonsAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalmaintainabilityCode Smelltype-dependentWhy is this an issue?In a Zen-like manner,NaNisn’t equal to anything, even itself. So comparisons (>, <, >=, <=) where one operand isNaNor evaluates toNaNalways returnfalse. Specifically,undefinedand objects that cannot be converted to numbers evaluate toNaNwhen used in numerical comparisons.This rule raises an issue when there is at least one path through the code where one of the operands to a comparison isNaN,undefinedor anObjectwhich cannot be converted to a number.Noncompliant code examplevar x; // x is currently "undefined" if (someCondition()) { x = 42; } if (42 > x) { // Noncompliant; "x" might still be "undefined" doSomething(); }
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: NaN in numeric comparison always evaluates to false. Use isNaN() instead.
NaN in numeric comparison always evaluates to false. Use isNaN() instead.
undefined literal in numeric comparison converts to NaN and always evaluates to false.
undefined literal in numeric comparison converts to NaN and always evaluates to false.
Variable declared without initialization is used in numeric comparison. Initialize the variable first.
Variable declared without initialization is used in numeric comparison. Initialize the variable first.
